content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:d6062bb9

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains extractable durable facts about the user's pet/content accounts and a reusable outreach plan plus strategic rationale for local pet-business collaboration.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements require resolving the stable namespace-level user entity; no existing candidates are available.
- mention=Luna; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=PERSON; name=Luna; reason=Luna is a recurring named pet and reusable entity; no existing candidates are available.
- mention=TikTok; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=SERVICE; name=TikTok; reason=TikTok is a concrete social media service discussed as a platform for the user's content.
- mention=Twitter; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=SERVICE; name=Twitter; reason=Twitter is a concrete social media service discussed as the user's account platform.
- mention=my video of Luna chasing a laser pointer; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=PRODUCT; name=User's TikTok video of Luna chasing a laser pointer; reason=The possessive wording identifies a reusable personal digital media item that later claims may use as an object.
- mention=my Twitter account; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=PRODUCT; name=User's Twitter account; reason=The user's specific account is a concrete durable digital item distinct from the Twitter service.
- mention=weekly Twitter chat on Thursdays; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=CONCEPT; name=Weekly Thursday social media marketing Twitter chat; reason=The recurring chat is a reusable event/community-like referent tied to follower-growth claims.
- mention=local pet businesses; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Local pet businesses; reason=The group is a reusable collaboration outreach target in the discussion, though not a specific named organization.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|Luna|TikTok|Twitter|local pet businesses
        text_queries=Luna chasing a laser pointer TikTok video 1456 views|Twitter account 187 followers weekly Thursday Twitter chat 7 new followers|local pet businesses collaboration outreach pet content promotions
        predicate_hints=has_goal|responsible_for|current_metric_value|metric_observation|has_constraint|prefers

        TARGET_MESSAGE source data:
        [external:provided-text:6f995cd64e9e489dbaf89e643d439a61] EXTERNAL
Source metadata: origin=provided_text; input_kind=TEXT; source_ref=longmemeval:d6062bb9:session:1; title=LongMemEval d6062bb9 session 1; user_consent_confirmed=true; force_memory_write=true; mode=force
LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/24 (Wed) 20:00
Transcript:
user: I'm trying to increase my online presence, and I was wondering if you could help me come up with some ideas for pet-centric content on TikTok, since my video of Luna chasing a laser pointer has been doing really well - it has 1,456 views.
assistant: Congratulations on your popular video! I'd be happy to help you come up with more pet-centric content ideas for TikTok. Here are a few ideas to get you started:

1. **Pet Tricks and Training**: Share tutorials on how to teach your pet new tricks, like "shake," "roll over," or "play dead." You could also showcase Luna's existing skills.
2. **Pet Playtime**: Create more videos like your laser pointer one, but try different toys or activities, such as playing fetch, tug-of-war, or hide-and-seek.
3. **Before and After Transformations**: Show the transformation of Luna's grooming process, from messy to clean and adorable.
4. **Pet and Owner Challenges**: Create fun challenges that you and Luna can do together, like a "treat toss" challenge or a "who can sit still the longest" contest.
5. **Pet's-Eye View**: Attach a camera to Luna's collar or harness and show the world from her perspective.
6. **Fun with Costumes**: Dress Luna up in cute costumes and create funny skits or scenarios.
7. **Pet Hacks and Tips**: Share useful tips and hacks for pet owners, such as how to keep your pet calm during thunderstorms or how to make homemade pet treats.
8. **Luna's Favorite Things**: Showcase Luna's favorite toys, treats, or activities, and explain why she loves them.
9. **Pet and Environment Interactions**: Record Luna interacting with her environment, such as playing with water, exploring outdoors, or reacting to different textures.
10. **Q&A Sessions**: Host Q&A sessions where you answer questions from followers about Luna, her breed, or pet care in general.
11. **Time-Lapse Videos**: Create time-lapse videos of Luna's daily activities, such as eating, sleeping, or playing.
12. **Pet Storytelling**: Create short, adorable stories about Luna's adventures, using captions and creative editing.
13. **Pet and Owner Bonding**: Share heartwarming moments of you and Luna bonding, such as cuddling, playing, or simply spending time together.
14. **Seasonal and Holiday Content**: Create seasonal or holiday-themed content, such as Luna in a Santa hat or playing in the snow.
15. **Collaborate with Other Pet Accounts**: Partner with other popular pet accounts on TikTok to create content, promote each other's accounts, and grow your audience.

Remember to always prioritize Luna's safety and comfort when creating content, and make sure to follow TikTok's community guidelines.

I hope these ideas inspire you to create more engaging and entertaining content for your audience!
user: That's a great list, thanks! I'll definitely try some of those out. I'm also wondering if you have any tips on how to increase engagement on my Twitter account, since I've been stuck at 187 followers for a while.
assistant: I'm happy to help you with that! Increasing engagement on Twitter can be a challenge, but with a solid strategy, you can grow your followers and boost interactions. Here are some tips to help you get unstuck:

1. **Optimize Your Profile**: Make sure your Twitter profile is complete, including a profile picture, header image, and bio that clearly states your name, interests, and what your account is about.
2. **Content Variety**: Post a mix of content types, such as:
	* Pet photos and videos (like your popular TikTok content)
	* Pet-related news, articles, or blog posts
	* Personal anecdotes and stories about Luna
	* Fun facts and trivia about pets
	* Behind-the-scenes content, like bloopers or sneak peeks
3. **Hashtag Strategy**: Research and use relevant hashtags in your tweets, such as #pets, #dogs, #petsofTwitter, #doglovers, etc. You can use tools like Hashtagify or RiteTag to find popular hashtags.
4. **Engage with Others**: Interact with other Twitter users by:
	* Responding to comments and messages promptly
	* Liking and retweeting content from other pet enthusiasts
	* Participating in Twitter Chats, like #PetChat or #DogChat
	* Hosting your own Twitter Chat or Q&A session
5. **Consistency is Key**: Post content regularly to keep your audience engaged. Aim for at least 3-5 tweets per week, but make sure you don't sacrifice quality for quantity.
6. **Visual Content**: Tweets with images or videos are more engaging than plain text. Use high-quality images, and consider creating Twitter-exclusive content.
7. **Ask Questions and Polls**: Encourage engagement by asking questions, creating polls, or hosting "Would you rather..." style tweets.
8. **Collaborate with Influencers**: Reach out to pet influencers or popular pet accounts on Twitter and collaborate on content, shoutouts, or even a takeover.
9. **Share User-Generated Content**: Share photos or stories from your followers, giving them credit and a shoutout. This encourages others to share their own content and interact with your account.
10. **Twitter Analytics**: Use Twitter Analytics to track your performance, identify what works, and adjust your strategy accordingly.
11. **Cross-Promote**: Share your Twitter content on other social media platforms, like Facebook and Instagram, to drive traffic to your Twitter account.
12. **Run a Contest**: Host a contest or giveaway to incentivize engagement and attract new followers. Partner with pet brands or influencers to increase visibility.
13. **Be Authentic and Patient**: Building a strong Twitter presence takes time. Be authentic, stay consistent, and don't get discouraged by slow growth.

By implementing these strategies, you should see an increase in engagement and followers on your Twitter account. Remember to track your progress, adjust your approach as needed, and always keep your content pet-centric and fun!
user: I've already started participating in a weekly Twitter chat on Thursdays, which focuses on social media marketing, and I've gained 7 new followers since joining. Do you think I should also try to connect with other pet owners and pet-related businesses in my area to see if we can collaborate on content or promotions?
assistant: That's a great start with the Twitter chat! Participating in online communities related to your niche can definitely help you grow your audience.

Connecting with local pet owners and pet-related businesses is an excellent idea. Collaborating with them can help you:

1. **Tap into a local audience**: Partnering with local businesses and pet owners can help you reach a more targeted audience that's interested in pet-related content and services.
2. **Create unique content**: You can collaborate on content ideas, such as:
	* Featuring local pet-friendly spots or events
	* Showcasing local pet businesses and their services
	* Sharing tips and advice from local pet experts
	* Hosting joint giveaways or contests
3. **Increase engagement**: By working with local pet owners and businesses, you can encourage them to share your content, participate in your Twitter chats, or even host a takeover.
4. **Build relationships**: Networking with local pet enthusiasts and businesses can lead to long-term relationships, potential partnerships, and even opportunities for sponsored content or collaborations.
5. **Support local pet community**: By working together, you can help promote the local pet community, support local businesses, and contribute to the overall well-being of pets in your area.

To get started, you can:

1. **Research local pet businesses**: Look for pet stores, groomers, trainers, veterinarians, and other pet-related services in your area.
2. **Reach out to local pet owners**: Attend local pet events, join Facebook groups or online forums for pet owners in your area, or connect with friends and family who have pets.
3. **Introduce yourself and propose collaboration**: Reach out to the businesses and pet owners you've identified, introduce yourself, and propose ways you can collaborate on content or promotions.
4. **Be clear about your goals and expectations**: Make sure you're transparent about what you're looking to achieve through the collaboration and what you can offer in return.
5. **Follow up and follow through**: After proposing a collaboration, be sure to follow up and follow through on your commitments.

Remember to always prioritize building genuine relationships and providing value to your collaborators. By doing so, you'll be more likely to create successful and long-term partnerships that benefit everyone involved.
user: I'm thinking of reaching out to local pet businesses to see if they'd be interested in collaborating on content or promotions. Do you think it would be a good idea to mention my popular TikTok video of Luna chasing a laser pointer to show my influence in the pet community?
assistant: Mentioning your popular TikTok video can be a great way to demonstrate your influence in the pet community, but you should consider the context and audience before doing so.

When reaching out to local pet businesses, you want to focus on how you can help them, rather than just showcasing your own achievements. Here's a suggestion:

**Tailor your approach**: Instead of leading with your TikTok video, start by introducing yourself, your pet-centric Twitter account, and your interest in collaborating with local pet businesses. Explain how you can help them reach a targeted audience, increase their online presence, or create engaging content.

**Highlight the benefits**: Emphasize the benefits of collaborating with you, such as:

* Reaching a local audience interested in pets
* Creating engaging content that resonates with pet owners
* Building brand awareness and credibility through social media
* Potential increase in foot traffic or sales through joint promotions

**Mention your TikTok video strategically**: If the conversation progresses and you've established a connection with the business owner, you can mention your popular TikTok video as an example of your content creation skills and your ability to engage with pet owners. This can help demonstrate your value as a collaborator.

Example:

"Hi [Business Owner], I'm [Your Name], a local pet enthusiast with a strong online presence on Twitter. I've been creating content around my pet, Luna, and have built a small but engaged audience. I'd love to explore ways we could collaborate on content or promotions to help increase your online visibility and reach local pet owners. As an example, I've had success with a TikTok video of Luna chasing a laser pointer, which has garnered over 1,400 views. I believe we could create similar engaging content together that would benefit both our audiences."

By taking a more collaborative and benefit-focused approach, you're more likely to pique the interest of local pet businesses and start a fruitful conversation.
user: That makes sense. I'll try to focus on the benefits of collaboration and highlight my ability to create engaging content. But I'm still a bit unsure about how to reach out to these businesses. Should I send them a direct message on social media, or is it better to send an email or even visit them in person?
assistant: Reaching out to local pet businesses can be done through various channels, and the best approach depends on your personal comfort level, the business's online presence, and the type of collaboration you're proposing. Here's a breakdown of the pros and cons of each approach:

**Direct Message (DM) on Social Media:**

Pros:

* Quick and easy to send
* Allows for a casual, conversational tone
* Can be a good way to initiate a conversation

Cons:

* May get lost in their DMs or notifications
* Limited space to elaborate on your proposal
* May not be taken as seriously as an email or in-person visit

**Email:**

Pros:

* More formal and professional than a DM
* Allows for a clear, detailed proposal and introduction
* Easier to attach files or links to examples of your work

Cons:

* May end up in their spam folder or get deleted
* Can be impersonal if not well-written or researched

**In-Person Visit:**

Pros:

* Personal touch and face-to-face interaction
* Can build rapport and establish a connection
* Allows for a more in-depth discussion and Q&A

Cons:

* Requires more time and effort
* May not be feasible if the business is busy or has limited staff
* Can be intimidating or uncomfortable for some people

Consider the following factors to decide which approach is best for you:

* **Business's online presence**: If the business is active on social media, a DM might be a good starting point. If they have a website with a contact form or email address, an email might be more suitable.
* **Type of collaboration**: If you're proposing a simple content collaboration, a DM or email might be sufficient. If you're proposing a more complex partnership or event, an in-person visit might be more appropriate.
* **Your comfort level**: If you're shy or uncomfortable with in-person interactions, an email or DM might be a better fit. If you're confident and enjoy meeting new people, an in-person visit could be a great way to build a connection.

Ultimately, you could try a combination of approaches. For example, you could send a DM or email to introduce yourself and propose a collaboration, and then follow up with an in-person visit if they express interest.

Remember to:

* Research the business and their online presence before reaching out
* Personalize your message and proposal to each business
* Be clear, concise, and respectful in your communication
* Be open to feedback and willing to adapt your approach as needed
user: I think I'll start by sending a brief introductory email to a few local pet businesses, highlighting the benefits of collaborating with me and mentioning my ability to create engaging content. I'll keep the tone professional and friendly, and attach some examples of my work, including my popular TikTok video of Luna chasing a laser pointer. If they express interest, I can follow up with a phone call or in-person meeting to discuss the details. Thanks for the advice!
assistant: That's a great plan! You're taking a professional and friendly approach, and by attaching examples of your work, you're showcasing your skills and creativity. By mentioning your popular TikTok video, you're also highlighting your ability to create engaging content that resonates with pet owners.

Remember to keep your email concise and to the point, focusing on the benefits of collaborating with you and how it can help their business. Make sure to proofread your email for grammar and spelling errors, and use a clear and compelling subject line that grabs their attention.

When you follow up with a phone call or in-person meeting, be prepared to discuss the details of the collaboration, including the type of content you can create, the frequency of posts, and the benefits of working together. Be open to their feedback and ideas, and be flexible in your approach.

Most importantly, be yourself, be enthusiastic, and show your passion for creating content that celebrates pets and pet owners. Your energy and creativity will shine through, and that can make all the difference in building a successful collaboration.

Good luck, and I hope you get a great response from the local pet businesses!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.
<env>
Project path: gromozeka-e2e-<id>

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

        Router reason: The transcript contains extractable durable facts about the user's pet/content accounts and a reusable outreach plan plus strategic rationale for local pet-business collaboration.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm trying to increase my online presence, and I was wondering if you could help me come up with some ideas for pet-centric content on TikTok, since my video of Luna chasing a laser pointer has been doing really well - it has 1,456 views.
assistant: Congratulations on your popular video! I'd be happy to help you come up with more pet-centric content ideas for TikTok. Here are a few ideas to get you started:

1. **Pet Tricks and Training**: Share tutorials on how to teach your pet new tricks, like "shake," "roll over," or "play dead." You could also showcase Luna's existing skills.
2. **Pet Playtime**: Create more videos like your laser pointer one, but try different toys or activities, such as playing fetch, tug-of-war, or hide-and-seek.
3. **Before and After Transformations**: Show the transformation of Luna's grooming process, from messy to clean and adorable.
4. **Pet and Owner Challenges**: Create fun challenges that you and Luna can do together, like a "treat toss" challenge or a "who can sit still the longest" contest.
5. **Pet's-Eye View**: Attach a camera to Luna's collar or harness and show the world from her perspective.
6. **Fun with Costumes**: Dress Luna up in cute costumes and create funny skits or scenarios.
7. **Pet Hacks and Tips**: Share useful tips and hacks for pet owners, such as how to keep your pet calm during thunderstorms or how to make homemade pet treats.
8. **Luna's Favorite Things**: Showcase Luna's favorite toys, treats, or activities, and explain why she loves them.
9. **Pet and Environment Interactions**: Record Luna interacting with her environment, such as playing with water, exploring outdoors, or reacting to different textures.
10. **Q&A Sessions**: Host Q&A sessions where you answer questions from followers about Luna, her breed, or pet care in general.
11. **Time-Lapse Videos**: Create time-lapse videos of Luna's daily activities, such as eating, sleeping, or playing.
12. **Pet Storytelling**: Create short, adorable stories about Luna's adventures, using captions and creative editing.
13. **Pet and Owner Bonding**: Share heartwarming moments of you and Luna bonding, such as cuddling, playing, or simply spending time together.
14. **Seasonal and Holiday Content**: Create seasonal or holiday-themed content, such as Luna in a Santa hat or playing in the snow.
15. **Collaborate with Other Pet Accounts**: Partner with other popular pet accounts on TikTok to create content, promote each other's accounts, and grow your audience.

Remember to always prioritize Luna's safety and comfort when creating content, and make sure to follow TikTok's community guidelines.

I hope these ideas inspire you to create more engaging and entertaining content for your audience!
user: That's a great list, thanks! I'll definitely try some of those out. I'm also wondering if you have any tips on how to increase engagement on my Twitter account, since I've been stuck at 187 followers for a while.
assistant: I'm happy to help you with that! Increasing engagement on Twitter can be a challenge, but with a solid strategy, you can grow your followers and boost interactions. Here are some tips to help you get unstuck:

1. **Optimize Your Profile**: Make sure your Twitter profile is complete, including a profile picture, header image, and bio that clearly states your name, interests, and what your account is about.
2. **Content Variety**: Post a mix of content types, such as:
	* Pet photos and videos (like your popular TikTok content)
	* Pet-related news, articles, or blog posts
	* Personal anecdotes and stories about Luna
	* Fun facts and trivia about pets
	* Behind-the-scenes content, like bloopers or sneak peeks
3. **Hashtag Strategy**: Research and use relevant hashtags in your tweets, such as #pets, #dogs, #petsofTwitter, #doglovers, etc. You can use tools like Hashtagify or RiteTag to find popular hashtags.
4. **Engage with Others**: Interact with other Twitter users by:
	* Responding to comments and messages promptly
	* Liking and retweeting content from other pet enthusiasts
	* Participating in Twitter Chats, like #PetChat or #DogChat
	* Hosting your own Twitter Chat or Q&A session
5. **Consistency is Key**: Post content regularly to keep your audience engaged. Aim for at least 3-5 tweets per week, but make sure you don't sacrifice quality for quantity.
6. **Visual Content**: Tweets with images or videos are more engaging than plain text. Use high-quality images, and consider creating Twitter-exclusive content.
7. **Ask Questions and Polls**: Encourage engagement by asking questions, creating polls, or hosting "Would you rather..." style tweets.
8. **Collaborate with Influencers**: Reach out to pet influencers or popular pet accounts on Twitter and collaborate on content, shoutouts, or even a takeover.
9. **Share User-Generated Content**: Share photos or stories from your followers, giving them credit and a shoutout. This encourages others to share their own content and interact with your account.
10. **Twitter Analytics**: Use Twitter Analytics to track your performance, identify what works, and adjust your strategy accordingly.
11. **Cross-Promote**: Share your Twitter content on other social media platforms, like Facebook and Instagram, to drive traffic to your Twitter account.
12. **Run a Contest**: Host a contest or giveaway to incentivize engagement and attract new followers. Partner with pet brands or influencers to increase visibility.
13. **Be Authentic and Patient**: Building a strong Twitter presence takes time. Be authentic, stay consistent, and don't get discouraged by slow growth.

By implementing these strategies, you should see an increase in engagement and followers on your Twitter account. Remember to track your progress, adjust your approach as needed, and always keep your content pet-centric and fun!
user: I've already started participating in a weekly Twitter chat on Thursdays, which focuses on social media marketing, and I've gained 7 new followers since joining. Do you think I should also try to connect with other pet owners and pet-related businesses in my area to see if we can collaborate on content or promotions?
assistant: That's a great start with the Twitter chat! Participating in online communities related to your niche can definitely help you grow your audience.

Connecting with local pet owners and pet-related businesses is an excellent idea. Collaborating with them can help you:

1. **Tap into a local audience**: Partnering with local businesses and pet owners can help you reach a more targeted audience that's interested in pet-related content and services.
2. **Create unique content**: You can collaborate on content ideas, such as:
	* Featuring local pet-friendly spots or events
	* Showcasing local pet businesses and their services
	* Sharing tips and advice from local pet experts
	* Hosting joint giveaways or contests
3. **Increase engagement**: By working with local pet owners and businesses, you can encourage them to share your content, participate in your Twitter chats, or even host a takeover.
4. **Build relationships**: Networking with local pet enthusiasts and businesses can lead to long-term relationships, potential partnerships, and even opportunities for sponsored content or collaborations.
5. **Support local pet community**: By working together, you can help promote the local pet community, support local businesses, and contribute to the overall well-being of pets in your area.

To get started, you can:

1. **Research local pet businesses**: Look for pet stores, groomers, trainers, veterinarians, and other pet-related services in your area.
2. **Reach out to local pet owners**: Attend local pet events, join Facebook groups or online forums for pet owners in your area, or connect with friends and family who have pets.
3. **Introduce yourself and propose collaboration**: Reach out to the businesses and pet owners you've identified, introduce yourself, and propose ways you can collaborate on content or promotions.
4. **Be clear about your goals and expectations**: Make sure you're transparent about what you're looking to achieve through the collaboration and what you can offer in return.
5. **Follow up and follow through**: After proposing a collaboration, be sure to follow up and follow through on your commitments.

Remember to always prioritize building genuine relationships and providing value to your collaborators. By doing so, you'll be more likely to create successful and long-term partnerships that benefit everyone involved.
user: I'm thinking of reaching out to local pet businesses to see if they'd be interested in collaborating on content or promotions. Do you think it would be a good idea to mention my popular TikTok video of Luna chasing a laser pointer to show my influence in the pet community?
assistant: Mentioning your popular TikTok video can be a great way to demonstrate your influence in the pet community, but you should consider the context and audience before doing so.

When reaching out to local pet businesses, you want to focus on how you can help them, rather than just showcasing your own achievements. Here's a suggestion:

**Tailor your approach**: Instead of leading with your TikTok video, start by introducing yourself, your pet-centric Twitter account, and your interest in collaborating with local pet businesses. Explain how you can help them reach a targeted audience, increase their online presence, or create engaging content.

**Highlight the benefits**: Emphasize the benefits of collaborating with you, such as:

* Reaching a local audience interested in pets
* Creating engaging content that resonates with pet owners
* Building brand awareness and credibility through social media
* Potential increase in foot traffic or sales through joint promotions

**Mention your TikTok video strategically**: If the conversation progresses and you've established a connection with the business owner, you can mention your popular TikTok video as an example of your content creation skills and your ability to engage with pet owners. This can help demonstrate your value as a collaborator.

Example:

"Hi [Business Owner], I'm [Your Name], a local pet enthusiast with a strong online presence on Twitter. I've been creating content around my pet, Luna, and have built a small but engaged audience. I'd love to explore ways we could collaborate on content or promotions to help increase your online visibility and reach local pet owners. As an example, I've had success with a TikTok video of Luna chasing a laser pointer, which has garnered over 1,400 views. I believe we could create similar engaging content together that would benefit both our audiences."

By taking a more collaborative and benefit-focused approach, you're more likely to pique the interest of local pet businesses and start a fruitful conversation.
user: That makes sense. I'll try to focus on the benefits of collaboration and highlight my ability to create engaging content. But I'm still a bit unsure about how to reach out to these businesses. Should I send them a direct message on social media, or is it better to send an email or even visit them in person?
assistant: Reaching out to local pet businesses can be done through various channels, and the best approach depends on your personal comfort level, the business's online presence, and the type of collaboration you're proposing. Here's a breakdown of the pros and cons of each approach:

**Direct Message (DM) on Social Media:**

Pros:

* Quick and easy to send
* Allows for a casual, conversational tone
* Can be a good way to initiate a conversation

Cons:

* May get lost in their DMs or notifications
* Limited space to elaborate on your proposal
* May not be taken as seriously as an email or in-person visit

**Email:**

Pros:

* More formal and professional than a DM
* Allows for a clear, detailed proposal and introduction
* Easier to attach files or links to examples of your work

Cons:

* May end up in their spam folder or get deleted
* Can be impersonal if not well-written or researched

**In-Person Visit:**

Pros:

* Personal touch and face-to-face interaction
* Can build rapport and establish a connection
* Allows for a more in-depth discussion and Q&A

Cons:

* Requires more time and effort
* May not be feasible if the business is busy or has limited staff
* Can be intimidating or uncomfortable for some people

Consider the following factors to decide which approach is best for you:

* **Business's online presence**: If the business is active on social media, a DM might be a good starting point. If they have a website with a contact form or email address, an email might be more suitable.
* **Type of collaboration**: If you're proposing a simple content collaboration, a DM or email might be sufficient. If you're proposing a more complex partnership or event, an in-person visit might be more appropriate.
* **Your comfort level**: If you're shy or uncomfortable with in-person interactions, an email or DM might be a better fit. If you're confident and enjoy meeting new people, an in-person visit could be a great way to build a connection.

Ultimately, you could try a combination of approaches. For example, you could send a DM or email to introduce yourself and propose a collaboration, and then follow up with an in-person visit if they express interest.

Remember to:

* Research the business and their online presence before reaching out
* Personalize your message and proposal to each business
* Be clear, concise, and respectful in your communication
* Be open to feedback and willing to adapt your approach as needed
user: I think I'll start by sending a brief introductory email to a few local pet businesses, highlighting the benefits of collaborating with me and mentioning my ability to create engaging content. I'll keep the tone professional and friendly, and attach some examples of my work, including my popular TikTok video of Luna chasing a laser pointer. If they express interest, I can follow up with a phone call or in-person meeting to discuss the details. Thanks for the advice!
assistant: That's a great plan! You're taking a professional and friendly approach, and by attaching examples of your work, you're showcasing your skills and creativity. By mentioning your popular TikTok video, you're also highlighting your ability to create engaging content that resonates with pet owners.

Remember to keep your email concise and to the point, focusing on the benefits of collaborating with you and how it can help their business. Make sure to proofread your email for grammar and spelling errors, and use a clear and compelling subject line that grabs their attention.

When you follow up with a phone call or in-person meeting, be prepared to discuss the details of the collaboration, including the type of content you can create, the frequency of posts, and the benefits of working together. Be open to their feedback and ideas, and be flexible in your approach.

Most importantly, be yourself, be enthusiastic, and show your passion for creating content that celebrates pets and pet owners. Your energy and creativity will shine through, and that can make all the difference in building a successful collaboration.

Good luck, and I hope you get a great response from the local pet businesses!
